During REM sleep, the brain is highly active, and most dreaming occurs. This stage is characterized by rapid eye movements, increased brain activity, and muscle paralysis, creating an environment conducive to vivid dreaming and mental rejuvenation.</p>\n    <p>Optimal REM sleep duration typically constitutes around 5-40% of total sleep time for most adults. However, individual needs can vary based on factors such as age, genetics, and lifestyle. While insufficient REM sleep can lead to cognitive impairment, mood disturbances, and memory deficits, excessive REM sleep may also indicate underlying sleep disorders or disruptions in sleep architecture.</p>\n    <p>To enhance the quality of REM sleep, consider incorporating relaxation techniques such as deep breathing exercises, meditation, or progressive muscle relaxation into your bedtime routine. Additionally, maintaining a consistent sleep schedule, avoiding caffeine and stimulating activities before bedtime, and creating a tranquil sleep environment can promote deeper and more restorative REM sleep.</p>\n    <p>By prioritizing the optimization of REM sleep, you can experience improved cognitive function, emotional well-being, and overall health.</p>\n    <p>Dream on and embrace the power of REM sleep for a truly revitalizing restorative experience!</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
        "deep_sleep": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Deep sleep</h1>\n    <p>Deep sleep, also known as slow-wave sleep (SWS), is a crucial stage of the sleep cycle that plays a vital role in physical restoration, immune function, and memory consolidation. This stage is characterized by slow brain waves, reduced muscle activity, and decreased heart rate and breathing rate.</p>\n    <p>During deep sleep, the body undergoes essential repair processes, including muscle growth and repair, tissue regeneration, and hormone regulation. Deep sleep is particularly important for physical recovery, energy restoration, and overall well-being.</p>\n    <p>For most adults, deep sleep typically constitutes around 5-40% of total sleep time, with the highest proportion occurring in the earlier part of the night. However, individual deep sleep needs can vary based on factors such as age, lifestyle, and overall health.</p>\n    <p>Insufficient deep sleep can lead to fatigue, impaired immune function, and increased risk of chronic health conditions such as obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Therefore, optimizing deep sleep duration and quality is crucial for overall health and vitality.</p>\n    <p>To promote deeper and more restorative deep sleep, establish a consistent sleep schedule, create a conducive sleep environment that is dark, quiet, and comfortable, and engage in relaxation techniques before bedtime. Avoiding caffeine, alcohol, and stimulating activities close to bedtime can also enhance deep sleep quality.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
        "latency": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Latency</h1>\n    <p>Latency measures how quickly you fall asleep after getting into bed, excluding any periods of wakefulness during the night. The optimal latency for most individuals falls between 5 to 20 minutes. Falling asleep within this timeframe indicates a smooth transition into sleep, reflecting healthy sleep patterns and readiness for restorative rest.</p>\n    <b>• Optimal (5-20 minutes):</b> Falling asleep within this range signifies an efficient and timely transition into sleep, indicative of good sleep hygiene and overall well-being.<br>\n    <b>• Pay attention (<5 minutes or >20 minutes):</b> Falling asleep in less than 5 minutes may suggest excessive tiredness or sleep deprivation, potentially indicating a need for more restful sleep. Ideally, this midtime should occur between 12 a.m. and 3 a.m., as this time frame aligns with the natural circadian rhythm of the body, facilitating deep, restorative sleep and enhanced recovery.</p>\n    <p>It is highly recommended to aim for a midtime between 12 a.m. and 3 a.m. to optimize sleep quality and overall well-being. Adjusting your bedtime routine to achieve this ideal midtime can significantly improve your sleep hygiene and contribute to better sleep outcomes.</p>\n    <p>Remember, prioritizing a good night's sleep not only ensures physical and mental rejuvenation but also sets the stage for a productive and fulfilling day ahead!</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",

The night time movement graph represents five-minute intervals as circular symbols, denoting movement intensity through different colors:</p>     • High-intensity movement: It suggests periods of likely wakefulness or frequent and rapid tossing and turning.<br>\n    • Medium-intensity movement: It indicates instances of probable tossing and turning during sleep.<br>\n    • Low-intensity movement: It signifies subtle shifts in sleep position.<br>\n  </body>\n</html>",
        "sleep_balance": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Sleep balance</h1>\n    <p>Sleep balance is a measure of your long-term sleep, in contrast to the sleep score, which measures your short-term sleep. Sleep balance compares your average sleep duration over the past 7 days to that of the past 14 days.</p>     <p>Most adults need 7-9 hours of sleep per night for optimal well-being.<b> Achieving optimal sleep balance involves consistently sleeping for 7-9 hours each night with minimal variation, ideally within 60 minutes.</b> For example, let's say you usually sleep 8 hours a night. If you consistently get 8 hours or close to it every night, your sleep balance would be in a good place. However, if you're only getting 4 or 5 hours most nights, you're building up a sleep debt because you're not meeting your body's sleep needs.</p>     <p>It is a useful indicator as it helps you understand if you're getting enough rest or if you might need to make some changes to improve your sleep habits.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
        "hr_graph": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Heart rate</h1>\n    <p>Luna continuously monitors your heart rate at 5-minute intervals during the night. For adults, the typical range for an average heart rate spans from 40 to 100 beats per minute, with lower values generally indicating superior cardiovascular health. In general, a lower resting heart rate is more favorable, and the objective is to maintain it within the normal range.</p>     <p>The ideal scenario is for your heart rate to reach its lowest point during the first half of your night's sleep, allowing your body ample time for rejuvenation and preparation for the day ahead. You can spot your lowest RHR point as the illuminated white dot on your graph.</p>     <p>Ideally, your heart rate graph during the night should resemble a gentle smile, beginning higher on the left side (near bedtime), descending toward the middle, and then gradually ascending on the right side (around your morning wake-up time).</p>     <p>If your heart rate isn't reaching its lowest point until the latter half of your night's sleep, this may signify that your body is undergoing increased stress or exertion during this time.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
        "hrv_graph": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Heart rate variability</h1>\n    <p>Heart Rate Variability (HRV) measures the variation in the time between two heartbeats while you are asleep. It is calculated by analyzing the intervals between consecutive heartbeats and serves as an indicator of how well your body can adapt to its environment and perform.</p>     <p>This metric is a highly personal measure, varying for each person based on factors such as age, gender, lifestyle, and fitness level. Rather than comparing oneself with others, it's more beneficial to compare your HRV to your own baseline values over time.</p>     <p>A high HRV indicates a healthy and responsive cardiovascular system, suggesting ease in adapting to stress and optimal fitness. Conversely, a lower-than-usual HRV may result from overtraining, inadequate sleep, or negative factors like stress.</p>\n    <p>As a rule of thumb:</p>\n    • Higher HRV is associated with rest-and-digest, general fitness, and good recovery.<br>\n    • Lower HRV is associated with fight-or-flight responses, stress, illness, or overtraining.<br>\n    <p>To improve your HRV, prioritize activities that promote relaxation and stress reduction, such as deep breathing exercises, meditation, and ensuring sufficient sleep.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
        "resting_hr_top": "<html>\n<body>\n    <h1>Average heart rate</h1>\n    <p>Average heart rate (HR) represents the average number of times your heart beats per minute while in a complete state of rest, typically measured during sleep when you are most relaxed. It is an important indicator of cardiovascular health as well as overall fitness and recovery.</p>\n    <p>The typical range for average heart rate in adults is between 40 and 100 beats per minute, with lower numbers generally indicating better cardiovascular health. A lower heart rate signifies a strong heart muscle capable of pumping more blood with each beat. Factors such as intense training, late meals, elevated body temperature, and strong emotions like excitement and stress can influence and keep your heart rate elevated. Regular exercise, a healthy diet, and stress management play key roles in improving your heart rate.</p>\n    <p>Monitoring your heart rate can help you track improvements in your cardiovascular fitness over time.</p>\n</body>\n</html>",
        "oxy_graph": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Average blood oxygen</h1>\n    <p>The Luna ring has the capability to monitor your blood oxygen saturation levels throughout your sleep, detecting both your overall oxygen saturation and any fluctuations.</p>     <p>These measurements provide valuable insights into your body's oxygen circulation and absorption efficiency, serving as a valuable indicator of your overall well-being and potentially revealing disturbances in your nighttime breathing.</p>     <p>It's worth noting that a normal oxygen saturation level falls within the range of 95% to 100%.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>"
    },

During REM sleep, the brain is highly active, and most dreaming occurs. This stage is characterized by rapid eye movements, increased brain activity, and muscle paralysis, creating an environment conducive to vivid dreaming and mental rejuvenation.</p>\n    <p>Optimal REM sleep duration typically constitutes around 5-40% of total sleep time for most adults. However, individual needs can vary based on factors such as age, genetics, and lifestyle. While insufficient REM sleep can lead to cognitive impairment, mood disturbances, and memory deficits, excessive REM sleep may also indicate underlying sleep disorders or disruptions in sleep architecture.</p>\n    <p>To enhance the quality of REM sleep, consider incorporating relaxation techniques such as deep breathing exercises, meditation, or progressive muscle relaxation into your bedtime routine. This stage is characterized by slow brain waves, reduced muscle activity, and decreased heart rate and breathing rate.</p>\n    <p>During deep sleep, the body undergoes essential repair processes, including muscle growth and repair, tissue regeneration, and hormone regulation. Deep sleep is particularly important for physical recovery, energy restoration, and overall well-being.</p>\n    <p>For most adults, deep sleep typically constitutes around 5-40% of total sleep time, with the highest proportion occurring in the earlier part of the night.         "hr_graph": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Heart rate</h1>\n    <p>Luna continuously monitors your heart rate at 5-minute intervals during the night. For adults, the typical range for an average heart rate spans from 40 to 100 beats per minute, with lower values generally indicating superior cardiovascular health. In general, a lower resting heart rate is more favorable, and the objective is to maintain it within the normal range.</p>     <p>The ideal scenario is for your heart rate to reach its lowest point during the first half of your night's sleep, allowing your body ample time for rejuvenation and preparation for the day ahead. You can spot your lowest RHR point as the illuminated white dot on your graph.</p>     <p>Ideally, your heart rate graph during the night should resemble a gentle smile, beginning higher on the left side (near bedtime), descending toward the middle, and then gradually ascending on the right side (around your morning wake-up time).</p>     <p>If your heart rate isn't reaching its lowest point until the latter half of your night's sleep, this may signify that your body is undergoing increased stress or exertion during this time.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
        "hrv_graph": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Heart rate variability</h1>\n    <p>Heart Rate Variability (HRV) measures the variation in the time between two heartbeats while you are asleep. It is calculated by analyzing the intervals between consecutive heartbeats and serves as an indicator of how well your body can adapt to its environment and perform.</p>     <p>This metric is a highly personal measure, varying for each person based on factors such as age, gender, lifestyle, and fitness level. Rather than comparing oneself with others, it's more beneficial to compare your HRV to your own baseline values over time.</p>     <p>A high HRV indicates a healthy and responsive cardiovascular system, suggesting ease in adapting to stress and optimal fitness. Conversely, a lower-than-usual HRV may result from overtraining, inadequate sleep, or negative factors like stress.</p>\n    <p>As a rule of thumb:</p>\n    • Higher HRV is associated with rest-and-digest, general fitness, and good recovery.<br>\n    • Lower HRV is associated with fight-or-flight responses, stress, illness, or overtraining.<br>\n    <p>To improve your HRV, prioritize activities that promote relaxation and stress reduction, such as deep breathing exercises, meditation, and ensuring sufficient sleep.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
